On `a:hover` / `a:focus-visible`, the border-bottom becomes transparent and `.nav-stroke` is shown with `filter: url(#nav-warp)` applied **For the footer**, links currently just have a simple `border-bottom` that goes full opacity on hover (lines 100-113). Should the component randomize/vary these, or accept them as attributes/slots? - The SVG filter (`#nav-warp`) is defined once in page-header. A component could include its own filter internally (using a unique ID to avoid collisions). - The component would essentially wrap or augment an `` tag. <script webc:bucket="components">
  function initDrawStrokes(selector, opts) {
    document.querySelectorAll(selector).forEach(function (el, i) {
      var svg = el.querySelector(".strokes");
      if (!svg) return;

      var interval;
      var filterId = (opts.filterId || "strokes-warp") + "-" + i;

      function draw() {
        drawStrokes(svg, Object.assign({}, opts, { filterId: filterId }));
      }

      function start() {
        draw();
        interval = setInterval(draw, 500);
      }

      function stop() {
        clearInterval(interval);
        svg.innerHTML = "";
      }

      el.addEventListener("mouseenter", start);
      el.addEventListener("mouseleave", stop);
      el.addEventListener("focusin", start);
      el.addEventListener("focusout", stop);
    });
  }

  function drawStrokes(svg, opts) {
    opts = opts || {};
    const container = svg.parentElement;
    const w = container.offsetWidth;
    const h = container.offsetHeight;
    const filterId = opts.filterId || "strokes-warp";

    svg.setAttribute("viewBox", `0 0 ${w} ${h}`);
    svg.innerHTML = "";

    const defs = document.createElementNS("http://www.w3.org/2000/svg", "defs");
    defs.innerHTML = `<filter id="${filterId}">
      <feTurbulence type="turbulence" baseFrequency="0.04" numOctaves="2" result="turbulence" seed="${Math.floor(Math.random() * 100)}" />
      <feDisplacementMap in="SourceGraphic" in2="turbulence" scale="3" xChannelSelector="R" yChannelSelector="G" />
    </filter>`;
    svg.appendChild(defs);

    const g = document.createElementNS("http://www.w3.org/2000/svg", "g");
    g.setAttribute("filter", `url(#${filterId})`);
    svg.appendChild(g);

    const margin = opts.margin || 5;
    let x = margin + Math.random() * (opts.startSpread || 10);

    while (x < w - margin) {
      const len = 15 + Math.random() * 60;
      const angle = (Math.random() - 0.5) * 2.8;
      const dx = Math.cos(angle) * len;
      const dy = Math.sin(angle) * len;

      let startY;
      if (opts.yBias === "edges") {
        startY =
          Math.random() < 0.5
            ? Math.random() * Math.random() * h * 0.5
            : h - Math.random() * Math.random() * h * 0.5;
      } else {
        startY = margin + Math.random() * (h - margin * 2);
      }

      const endX = x + dx;
      const endY = startY + dy;
      const t = 0.3 + Math.random() * 0.4;
      const mx = x + dx * t + (Math.random() - 0.5) * 12;
      const my = startY + dy * t + (Math.random() - 0.5) * 12;
      const d = `M${x} ${startY}L${mx} ${my}L${endX} ${endY}`;

      const path = document.createElementNS(
        "http://www.w3.org/2000/svg",
        "path",
      );
      path.setAttribute("d", d);
      path.setAttribute("stroke", "currentColor");
      path.setAttribute("stroke-width", 2 + Math.random() * 4);
      path.setAttribute("stroke-linecap", "butt");
      path.setAttribute("fill", "none");
      g.appendChild(path);

      x += 5 + Math.random() * 12;
    }
  }
</script>

<script webc:type="render" webc:is="template">
  module.exports = function () {
    const seededRandom = (seed) => {
      let s = seed | 0;
      return () => {
        s = (s * 1103515245 + 12345) & 0x7fffffff;
        return s / 0x7fffffff;
      };
    };

    const pointsToPath = (coords, breaks) => {
      const n = coords.length;
      if (n < 2) return "";
      const f = (v) => v.toFixed(2);

      let d = `M ${f(coords[0][0])} ${f(coords[0][1])}`;
      for (let i = 1; i < n; i++) {
        const cmd = breaks.has(i) ? "M" : "L";
        d += ` ${cmd} ${f(coords[i][0])} ${f(coords[i][1])}`;
      }
      return d;
    };

    const year = parseInt(this.year) || 2024;
    const count = parseInt(this.count) || 8;
    const width = parseInt(this.width) || 250;
    const height = parseInt(this.height) || 25;

    const rng = seededRandom(year * 100 + count);
    const numPoints = 15;
    const numLines = 3 + Math.floor(count * 0.4);

    const pad = 2;
    const numFrames = 8;

    const paths = [];
    for (let i = 0; i < numLines; i++) {
      const startY = rng() * height;
      const endY = rng() * height * 0.1;

      // Pre-compute the fixed x and baseY for each point
      const base = [];
      for (let j = 0; j <= numPoints; j++) {
        const t = j / numPoints;
        const x = pad + t * (width - pad * 2);
        const baseY = startY + (endY - startY) * t;
        const spread = 1 - t * 0.8;
        base.push({ x, baseY, spread });
      }

      // Pick random break points for this line
      const numBreaks = 1 + Math.floor(rng() * 3);
      const breaks = new Set();
      while (breaks.size < numBreaks) {
        const idx = 2 + Math.floor(rng() * (numPoints - 3));
        breaks.add(idx);
      }

      // Generate wobble variations
      const frames = [];
      for (let f = 0; f < numFrames; f++) {
        const coords = base.map(({ x, baseY, spread }, j) => {
          if (j === 0 || j === numPoints) return [x, baseY];
          const wobble = (rng() - 0.5) * height * 0.45 * spread;
          const y = Math.max(pad, Math.min(height - pad, baseY + wobble));
          return [x, y];
        });
        frames.push(pointsToPath(coords, breaks));
      }

      const strokeWidth = (0.3 + rng() * 0.5).toFixed(2);
      const dur = (2 + rng() * 3).toFixed(1);
      const values = [...frames, frames[0]].join(";");
      paths.push(
        `<path d="${frames[0]}" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="${strokeWidth}"><animate attributeName="d" values="${values}" dur="${dur}s" calcMode="discrete" repeatCount="indefinite" /></path>`,
      );
    }

    const filterId = `scratch-warp-${year}`;
    const seed = Math.floor(rng() * 100);
    const defs = `<defs><filter id="${filterId}"><feTurbulence type="turbulence" baseFrequency="0.04" numOctaves="2" result="turbulence" seed="${seed}" /><feDisplacementMap in="SourceGraphic" in2="turbulence" scale="3" xChannelSelector="R" yChannelSelector="G" /></filter></defs>`;

    return `<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 ${width} ${height}" width="${width}" height="${height}" aria-hidden="true">${defs}<g filter="url(#${filterId})">${paths.join("")}</g></svg>`;
  };
</script>

content.text:
I see the changes you made — you simplified it down to one path per link (removed the second path), changed stroke-width to a consistent `1.4`, and switched from `display: none`/`display: block` toggling to `opacity: 0`/`opacity: 1`.

Here's what I see as abstractable:

**SVG markup** — every `.nav-stroke` SVG is identical except for the path `d` and `animate values`. The wrapper (`viewBox="0 0 40 10"`, `aria-hidden="true"`, `preserveAspectRatio="none"`) and the path attributes (`fill="none"`, `stroke="currentColor"`, `stroke-width="1.4"`) are the same every time.

**SVG filter** — the `#nav-warp` filter definition (lines 20-44) needs to exist once on the page. Both header and footer would need it. Could live in the layout or be its own tiny component.

**CSS** — the `.nav-stroke` positioning/visibility styles (lines 174-192) are the reusable bit:
- Absolute positioning below the link (`bottom: -2px`, `left: 0`, `width: 100%`, `height: 6px`)
- `filter: url(#nav-warp)`
- `opacity: 0` by default, `opacity: 1` on hover/focus
- The parent `<a>` needs `position: relative` and the `border-bottom-color: transparent` swap on hover

**What varies** — just the path `d` values and `dur` timings. That's really it.

So the main candidates for a shared piece:
1. **The filter SVG** — extract to its own component or the layout so it's available everywhere
2. **The `.nav-stroke` CSS** — could go in a global/shared stylesheet since it's the same rules
3. **The SVG markup** — if JS is generating the paths (randomizing), then you'd just need a JS function that creates the SVG element and appends it to any `<a>`

Does that match what you're seeing? Want me to go in a specific direction with any of these?

content.text:
OK, I've got the full picture. Here's my plan:

1. **Layout-main**: Add a global SVG defs block in `<body>` with the filter, renamed to something like `svg-filter-warp` 
2. **tylergaw.css**: Add `.nav-stroke` styles in the `components` layer
3. **page-header**: Remove the inline filter SVG and the `.nav-stroke` CSS rules (keep only the header-specific nav styling), update `url()` reference to new filter ID

Before I start — what do you want the filter ID to be? Something like `svg-filter-warp`? And for the global SVG defs element, any preference on where in `<body>` it goes (top, right after `<page-header>`, etc.)?

message.content:
nav-item-warp, put it right after the opening <body>

So wait, there will be no abstracted svg markup?

content.text:
Right, good point. The SVG markup itself is still repeated for each link — the `<svg class="nav-stroke">` with its `<path>` and `<animate>`. Each one has unique `d` values and `dur` timings though, so there's not a clean way to template that without JS or a WebC render function.

The things we *can* abstract are the filter and the CSS. That means when you add strokes to footer links, you'd still write out the SVG markup per link, but you wouldn't need to redefine the filter or the positioning/visibility CSS.

Is that acceptable, or do you want to explore a WebC render component (like scratch-lines) that generates the path markup from minimal input?

content.thinking:
The user wants to know what it would look like to generate the `d` and `values` attributes programmatically instead of hardcoding them.

Looking at the current patterns:

**`d` attribute** (initial path shape): A series of line segments across a 40x10 viewBox. Always starts at x=0 and ends at x=40, with ~5-6 points in between. Y values range from ~1 to ~8.5. Example: `M0 5 L6 2 L14 7 L22 1.5 L30 6.5 L40 3`
**`values` attribute** (animate keyframes): 4 variations of the same kind of path, separated by semicolons. Each frame has the same number of points but different x/y positions.

The approach would be to generate these dynamically: pick an interior point count, space the x values roughly evenly across the 0-40 range with some randomness, randomize y values between 1 and 9, create 4 animation frames that way for the `values` attribute, and use the first frame as the initial `d`. This logic could live in the WebC render function.

I could leverage the same pattern that scratch-lines already uses, maybe accepting a `seed` prop for deterministic randomness or keeping it simpler with no props at all.

<script webc:type="render" webc:is="template">
module.exports = function () {
  const seed = parseInt(this.seed) || 1;
  let s = seed | 0;
  const rand = () => {
    s = (s * 1103515245 + 12345) & 0x7fffffff;
    return s / 0x7fffffff;
  };

  const randY = () => (1 + rand() * 8).toFixed(1);

  const generatePath = () => {
    const pts = [`M0 ${randY()}`];
    for (let i = 1; i <= 4; i++) {
      const x = (i / 5) * 40 + (rand() - 0.5) * 6;
      pts.push(`L${x.toFixed(1)} ${randY()}`);
    }
    pts.push(`L40 ${randY()}`);
    return pts.join(" ");
  };

  const d = generatePath();
  const values = [d, generatePath(), generatePath(), generatePath()].join(";");
  const dur = (0.4 + rand() * 0.2).toFixed(2) + "s";

  return `<svg class="nav-stroke" viewBox="0 0 40 10" aria-hidden="true" preserveAspectRatio="none"><path d="${d}" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.4"><animate attributeName="d" calcMode="discrete" repeatCount="indefinite" dur="${dur}" values="${values}" /></path></svg>`;
};
</script>

content.text:
Any integer. Different numbers produce different-looking paths because the seeded random number generator produces a deterministic sequence from each seed. So `seed="1"` always generates the same paths, `seed="2"` always generates different but consistent paths, etc.

It's the same approach scratch-lines uses — keeps the output stable across builds while making each instance visually distinct.
